narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 46.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction draws from your academic record, like being in the 5th decile for Year 1 grades—slightly below the typical student's 6th decile—and especially your 4th decile in Year 3 grades, well under the average 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 34 also falls short of the usual 37, while your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 edges above the average 3.2, and your middle family income aligns with many applicants. As a white male planning part-time work, you're in the majority for gender and race but less common for part-time status.

The model's decision hinged most on your Year 3 decile ranking, which strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting bar failure since it was lower than most students'. Next in influence came your Year 1 decile, similarly pulling toward a fail prediction due to being below average. Your LSAT score ranked third, again contributing to the risk because it's under the norm for successful bar takers.

Family income followed closely, with your middle bracket slightly increasing the predicted risk compared to higher earners who tend to pass more often. Your racial background as white had a modest push toward failure in this case, though it's the most common group. Your solid UGPA and part-time plans each played smaller roles in tipping toward risk, while being male provided a tiny boost toward predicting success.

Overall, your profile shows strengths like a better-than-average UGPA, but the below-average early grades, especially in Year 3, and lower LSAT overshadowed them relative to peers who pass the bar. To boost your future chances, focus on strengthening later academic performance—perhaps through targeted study habits or extra courses to climb those deciles. Retaking the LSAT for a score closer to 37 could make a big difference, as could exploring full-time study options if feasible, since part-timers face higher predicted hurdles. With these realistic steps, you could shift your profile toward the successful majority and reapply stronger. Keep pushing—you've already shown UGPA potential that stands out.
